fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need a CLI tool for working with IPv6 network blocks — basically a dev utility that our infrastructure folks can pipe JSON into and get structured text back. Think of it like that subnet calculator we built for the IPv4 stuff last quarter, but IPv6 only this time. The tool needs to handle the usual stuff: parsing network blocks, figuring out range boundaries, converting between the mask and prefix-length forms, checking if an address falls inside a network, splitting blocks into smaller chunks, and merging adjacent blocks back together. Also needs to gracefully handle bad input — our old tool would just crash and spit out Python tracebacks which broke the pipeline scripts downstream, so we need normalized error output this time regardless of what language we pick. One thing that kept coming up in retro: the numeric representation thing — apparently some of our allocation scripts need the raw integer value of an address for database storage. Oh and there was a request from the routing team about being able to "guess" a reasonable prefix for a bare address input. The whole thing should read a JSON blob from stdin and write plain key=value lines to stdout. Keep it clean, no extra logging noise on stdout. Architecture-wise, follow the same separation pattern we used on the last network utility — don't cram everything into one file.

trigger_keywords: how to report broadcast in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: IPv6 does not use broadcast addresses. The broadcast field must always be reported as the literal string `null` (not omitted, not empty). Example: `broadcast=null`

trigger_keywords: how to count usable vs total
difference between total and usable for IPv6
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For IPv6 networks, usable equals total — there is no concept of reserving network/broadcast addresses. A /128 has total=1 and usable=1; a /127 has total=2 and usable=2. Do not subtract 2 as you would for IPv4.

trigger_keywords: what to print on bad input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Errors must be rendered as two key=value lines: `error=<category>` and `param=<value or null>`. The three valid categories are: `invalid_argument` (malformed/unparseable input), `argument_null` (null/missing required value), and `argument_out_of_range` (value outside valid range, e.g. prefix > 128). When no specific param name is available, use `param=null`.

The integer output must be an unsigned decimal integer — no hex, no `0x` prefix, no language object repr. IPv6 addresses are 128-bit so the value can exceed 64-bit range; use arbitrary-precision integers. Output format is `parsed=true\ninteger=<decimal>\n`.

All IPv6 addresses and networks in output must be in compressed lowercase notation per RFC 5952 (e.g. `2001:db8::` not `2001:0DB8:0000:...`). The canonical field for a network is `<compressed-network-address>/<prefix>`, e.g. `2001:db8::/64`.

trigger_keywords: IPv4 address in IPv6 network check
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Different address families are never contained. If the network is IPv6 and the address is IPv4 (or vice versa), the result must be `contains=false` — do not attempt any mapping or conversion.

Subnetting output starts with `count=<total child networks>`, followed by one line per requested index in the form `subnet[<index>]=<canonical network>`. Only the indices explicitly requested in the `indices` array are printed. When `try_mode=true` and the request is invalid, output `subnetted=false\ncount=null\n` instead of raising.

For two-network input, output `supernetted=<true|false>
supernet=<canonical|null>
`. Two networks can be merged if they are adjacent siblings at the same prefix length (result is parent /prefix-1) OR if one is nested inside the other (result is the containing network). If neither condition holds, output `supernetted=false
supernet=null
`.

For `prefix_text` input: parse the string as an integer; if valid (0–128) output `parsed=true
cidr=<n>
`, otherwise `parsed=false
cidr=null
`. For bare `address` input: guess a default prefix (typically /128 for a host address) and output `parsed=true
cidr=<n>
`.

Netmask validation output is two lines: `valid=<true|false>` (true only if bits are contiguous from MSB with no gaps) and `bits_set=<decimal>` (total number of 1-bits regardless of contiguity). A mask like `ffff:...:fff0` is valid=true with bits_set=124.

trigger_keywords: how does adapter decide which feature
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter dispatches based on the keys present in the JSON object from stdin: `notation` key → Feature 1/2 (CIDR string parse); `address`+`netmask` → Feature 1 (network summary); `prefix` integer → Feature 3.1; `netmask` alone → Feature 3.2/3.3; `address` alone → Feature 4 or Feature 8; `network`+`address` → Feature 5 containment; `network`+`other_network` → Feature 5 overlap; `network`+`new_prefix` → Feature 6 subnetting; `networks` array → Feature 7 supernetting; `prefix_text` → Feature 8.

The test harness is invoked as `bash rcb_tests/test.sh [--cases-dir <subdir>]`. Default cases-dir is `test_cases`. For each case in `feature<N>_<name>.json`, stdout is written to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/feature<N>_<name>@<ZZZ>.txt` where ZZZ is zero-padded 3-digit case index. Output files contain only raw stdout — no PASS/FAIL metadata.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same separation pattern we used on the last network utility
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Multi-file directory structure with strict separation: core domain logic (src/), I/O adapter (adapter or cli entry point), and tests (rcb_tests/) — mirroring the architecture described in start.md sections on Scale-Driven Code Organization and Separation of Concerns

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The cross-address-family containment check in Feature 5: IPv4 addresses passed to an IPv6 network containment check must return contains=false without error, as described in feature5_ipv6_containment_and_overlap.json case 3

context_pointers.vague_reference: the numeric representation thing for database storage
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4 (IPv6 Address Numeric Conversion): converting IPv6 address strings to unsigned 128-bit decimal integers, output as `parsed=true\ninteger=<decimal>\n`, defined in feature4_ipv6_address_numbers.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: our old tool would just crash and spit out Python tracebacks which broke the pipeline scripts
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2 error normalization: all exceptions must be caught and rendered as `error=<category>\nparam=<value|null>\n` using only the three categories: invalid_argument, argument_null, argument_out_of_range — defined in feature2_ipv6_parse_errors.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect of the requirement. Please check the existing network utility patterns in the codebase and align with the key=value stdout contract — when in doubt, look at how the IPv4 tool handled similar edge cases and apply the same normalization approach.

feature1_ipv6_network_summary.json.description: Parse IPv6 network inputs and report the normalized network range fields, including canonical notation, range endpoints, prefix length, and address counts.

cases.expected_output:
canonical=2001:db8::/128
network=2001:db8::
netmask=ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff
broadcast=null
first_usable=2001:db8::
last_usable=2001:db8::
cidr=128
total=1
usable=1

canonical=2001:db8::/127
network=2001:db8::
netmask=ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:fffe
broadcast=null
first_usable=2001:db8::
last_usable=2001:db8::1
cidr=127
total=2
usable=2

feature2_ipv6_parse_errors.json.description: Reject malformed IPv6 network inputs and out-of-range prefix requests using language-neutral error categories.

feature3_1_ipv6_prefix_to_netmask.json.description: Convert IPv6 prefix lengths to their contiguous netmask address representation.

feature3_2_ipv6_netmask_to_prefix.json.description: Convert valid contiguous IPv6 netmasks to prefix lengths while reporting whether conversion succeeded.

feature3_3_ipv6_netmask_validation.json.description: Validate whether an IPv6 mask is contiguous from the most significant bit and report the number of set bits.

feature4_ipv6_address_numbers.json.description: Interpret IPv6 addresses as unsigned integer values and expose successful conversion with the decimal result.

feature5_ipv6_containment_and_overlap.js.description: Determine whether an IPv6 network contains an address or another network, and whether two networks overlap.

feature6_ipv6_subnetting.json.description: Split a network into child networks of a longer prefix and expose count plus requested indexed children.

feature7_ipv6_supernetting.json.description: Combine adjacent or nested networks where possible and report whether a supernet was produced.

feature8_ipv6_guess_and_parse_prefix.jso.description: Parse textual IPv6 prefix lengths and guess the default prefix length for bare IPv6 addresses.
